Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: verbal idiomatic clause with motion verb + prepositional phrase
Figurative Meaning: to make a mess of something or ruin a situation through foolish action
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: sbagliar gravemente o rovinare una situazione
Usage Contexts: daily speech, humor, sarcasm, workplace conversations
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common Venetian humorous expression referencing the word 'mona', historically used in Veneto as a mild insult indicating foolishness.
First Example Sentence: Se continui cusì te finirà col andar in mona tuto el progetto.
Second Example Sentence: I gà provà a sistemar el computer, ma i ga fato andar tuto in mona.
Third Example Sentence: Par no far andar in mona la serata, gavemo cambià programma.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: noun phrase metaphor with possession verb
Figurative Meaning: to have extremely sharp observation skills
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: avere vista molto acuta o grande capacità di osservazione
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Falcons symbolize sharp vision in Mediterranean folklore and hunting traditions.
First Example Sentence: El capo ga un ocio de falco e el nota ogni minimo errore.
Second Example Sentence: Co Lucia che controla i conti no scapa gnente: la ga proprio un ocio de falco.
Third Example Sentence: Par far el restaurador bisogna aver un ocio de falco.

Idiom (Venetian): ciapar do piconi con na fava
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: verbal idiom with transitive verb phrase
Figurative Meaning: to achieve two goals with one action
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: ottenere due risultati con una sola azione
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Variant of a widespread European proverb about killing two birds with one stone, adapted in Venetian rural imagery.
First Example Sentence: Se andemo in centro par lavorar e comprar, ciapemo do piconi con na fava.
Second Example Sentence: Con sto viaggio go ciapà do piconi con na fava: lavoro e vacanza.
Third Example Sentence: El corso online me fa ciapar do piconi con na fava: studio e pratica.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: verbal idiom with infinitive verb + object phrase
Figurative Meaning: to add more elements to an already complex situation
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: aggiungere altri problemi o elementi alla situazione
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Originates from traditional Venetian cooking where adding more meat increased complexity of the dish.
First Example Sentence: No meter altra carne al fogo adesso che la situazion xe già complicada.
Second Example Sentence: Durante la riunion el direttor ga metù altra carne al fogo.
Third Example Sentence: Se continui a parlar cusì te meti solo altra carne al fogo.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: verbal idiom with verb + definite article + adjective used substantively
Figurative Meaning: to act sly or manipulative for personal advantage
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: comportarsi in modo furbo o opportunista
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common Venetian and Italian social expression criticizing opportunistic behavior.
First Example Sentence: No far el furbo e paga la to parte.
Second Example Sentence: El ga provà a far el furbo col contratto.
Third Example Sentence: In sto ufficio chi prova a far el furbo vien subito scoperto.

Figurative Meaning: do not tell lies or excuses
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Bale' historically refers to exaggerated stories told in Venetian markets and taverns.
First Example Sentence: No vegnir a contarme bale: so ben cossa xe sucesso.
Second Example Sentence: Quando el fioł prova a scusarse, la mama ghe dis: no vegnir a contarme bale.
Third Example Sentence: Dopo tuto quel che xe sucesso, no vegnir a contarme bale.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: fixed verbal idiom with infinitive verb phrase
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Metaphor referring to becoming as still as a stone from surprise.
First Example Sentence: Quando go visto el risultato so restà de sasso.
Second Example Sentence: La notizia ga fato restar de sasso tuto el ufficio.
Third Example Sentence: I xe restai de sasso davanti a quel spettacolo.

Figurative Meaning: to spend money quickly or wastefully
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from imagery of money falling through holes in one's hands.
First Example Sentence: Marco ga le man buche e el spende tuto el stipendio.
Second Example Sentence: Se continui cusì par che ti gabi le man buche.
Third Example Sentence: Chi ga le man buche no riese mai a risparmiar.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: verbal idiom with infinitive + object phrase
Figurative Meaning: to keep things going despite difficulties
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Originates from traveling entertainers maintaining their small show booths despite hardship.
First Example Sentence: Con pochi soldi i ga tirà avanti la baraca par ani.
Second Example Sentence: In azienda gavemo da tirar avanti la baraca finché riva el finanziamento.
Third Example Sentence: La fameja tira avanti la baraca con tanti sacrifici.

Figurative Meaning: to speak without being listened to
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Wind imagery appears frequently in Venetian maritime culture representing lost words.
First Example Sentence: Quando spiego e nisun ascolta me par de parlar al vento.
Second Example Sentence: La maestra se stufa de parlar al vento co i studenti distrati.
Third Example Sentence: Se no prestè atension, mi parlo al vento.
